texts.label: text
texts.orig: However,  several  drawbacks  have  to  be  addressed  before  the  industrial exploitation of these super glassy polymers. For instance, TR polymers have low solubility in common solvents for membrane preparation,  which,  together  with  their  high  cost  and  the  need  for  high thermal  treatment  temperatures,  have  been  bottlenecks  for  their industrialization. PIM membranes and also perfluoro polymers exhibit other issues, such as moderate selectivity and low stability over time, i. e., severe physical aging, especially when the membrane is made in the TFC form [78,189].
texts.text: However,  several  drawbacks  have  to  be  addressed  before  the  industrial exploitation of these super glassy polymers. For instance, TR polymers have low solubility in common solvents for membrane preparation,  which,  together  with  their  high  cost  and  the  need  for  high thermal  treatment  temperatures,  have  been  bottlenecks  for  their industrialization. PIM membranes and also perfluoro polymers exhibit other issues, such as moderate selectivity and low stability over time, i. e., severe physical aging, especially when the membrane is made in the TFC form [78,189].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2.3. Facilitated transport membranes
texts.text: 3.2.3. Facilitated transport membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Facilitated transport membranes have been intensively studied for CO2  separation. Both FSC and mobile carrier-based membranes have been developed to take advantage of CO2 as an acidic, reactive gas. Some of  the  representative  CO2-facilitated  transport  carriers  are  shown  in Fig. 10 [190,191]. Several mathematical models have also been developed to characterize the facilitated transport effect in FTMs [192].
texts.text: Facilitated transport membranes have been intensively studied for CO2  separation. Both FSC and mobile carrier-based membranes have been developed to take advantage of CO2 as an acidic, reactive gas. Some of  the  representative  CO2-facilitated  transport  carriers  are  shown  in Fig. 10 [190,191]. Several mathematical models have also been developed to characterize the facilitated transport effect in FTMs [192].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polyvinyamine  (PVAm)  is  one  of  the  representative  amine-based polymers  for  FTMs.  As  the  polymer  with  the  highest  content  of  primary amino groups, PVAm is a natural choice for FTMs to exploit the CO2-amine  chemistry.  However,  the  firstly  reported  CO2  permeance through PVAm membranes had been low ( < 10 GPU), not reflecting the facilitated transport effects[193,194]. Later on, the presence of water was found critical as it is actively involved in the reaction to enhance CO2 transport and achieve high CO2 permeance [195,196]. The strong dependence of FTMs ' performance on the feed gas relative humidity (RH), or more precisely, water uptake in the membrane, was reported by Deng  et  al.  [195,196].  As  water  is  involved  in  the  CO2-facilitated transport reactions and CO2 transport in FTMs is enhanced by the water taken  up  within  the  membrane  matrix,  FTM  membranes  are  usually made of hydrophilic polymers with a high degree of water swelling. For FTM membranes, separation under humid conditions must be ensured when designing a membrane module or a separation process [197,198].
texts.text: Polyvinyamine  (PVAm)  is  one  of  the  representative  amine-based polymers  for  FTMs.  As  the  polymer  with  the  highest  content  of  primary amino groups, PVAm is a natural choice for FTMs to exploit the CO2-amine  chemistry.  However,  the  firstly  reported  CO2  permeance through PVAm membranes had been low ( < 10 GPU), not reflecting the facilitated transport effects[193,194]. Later on, the presence of water was found critical as it is actively involved in the reaction to enhance CO2 transport and achieve high CO2 permeance [195,196]. The strong dependence of FTMs ' performance on the feed gas relative humidity (RH), or more precisely, water uptake in the membrane, was reported by Deng  et  al.  [195,196].  As  water  is  involved  in  the  CO2-facilitated transport reactions and CO2 transport in FTMs is enhanced by the water taken  up  within  the  membrane  matrix,  FTM  membranes  are  usually made of hydrophilic polymers with a high degree of water swelling. For FTM membranes, separation under humid conditions must be ensured when designing a membrane module or a separation process [197,198].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PVAm membranes were documented with extraordinarily high CO2 permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity under fully humid conditions[58]. PVAm  can  be  blended  with  other  compatible  polymers  (e.g.,  polyvinylalcohol (PVA)) to improve the membrane properties or embedding nano additives (e.g., nanocellulose, carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene oxide (GO) and TiO2) to further increase its gas separation properties [195,199-202]. Wang and coworkers have developed PVAm-based copolymers as FSC membranes, such as by introducing functional groups into PVAm main chains, and both CO2 permeability and selectivity with long-term stability were documented [203,204]. It is worth noting that by proper combination and optimization of mobile carrier and nanofillers, ultrahigh CO2/N2  selectivity(~1000) and high CO2  permeance (~2400 GPU) can be simultaneously obtained [205]. In another study, FT membranes with nickel-dope-hindered tertiary amine-ring structure were employed to separate CO2, ultrahigh CO2 permeance (8960 GPU) coupled with modurate CO2/N2 selectivity(40) was documented [206].
texts.text: PVAm membranes were documented with extraordinarily high CO2 permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity under fully humid conditions[58]. PVAm  can  be  blended  with  other  compatible  polymers  (e.g.,  polyvinylalcohol (PVA)) to improve the membrane properties or embedding nano additives (e.g., nanocellulose, carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene oxide (GO) and TiO2) to further increase its gas separation properties [195,199-202]. Wang and coworkers have developed PVAm-based copolymers as FSC membranes, such as by introducing functional groups into PVAm main chains, and both CO2 permeability and selectivity with long-term stability were documented [203,204]. It is worth noting that by proper combination and optimization of mobile carrier and nanofillers, ultrahigh CO2/N2  selectivity(~1000) and high CO2  permeance (~2400 GPU) can be simultaneously obtained [205]. In another study, FT membranes with nickel-dope-hindered tertiary amine-ring structure were employed to separate CO2, ultrahigh CO2 permeance (8960 GPU) coupled with modurate CO2/N2 selectivity(40) was documented [206].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several types of amine-based FSC membranes using polymers other than  PVAm  have  been  investigated,  including  polyallylamine  [207], polyethyleneimine [65,208-210], and polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer. Tertiary amines were also utilized as fixed-site carriers in FTMs as they offer sterically hindering effects for higher facilitated transport efficiency and hence better CO2 separation properties [211-215] (Fig. 10 (a) ).
texts.text: Several types of amine-based FSC membranes using polymers other than  PVAm  have  been  investigated,  including  polyallylamine  [207], polyethyleneimine [65,208-210], and polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer. Tertiary amines were also utilized as fixed-site carriers in FTMs as they offer sterically hindering effects for higher facilitated transport efficiency and hence better CO2 separation properties [211-215] (Fig. 10 (a) ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Small CO2-philic or reactive molecules ( Fig. 10 (b)) , including amino acid salts, ionic liquids, mimic enzymes, and KOH, have been used as mobile  carriers  and  added  into  a  non-reactive  hydrophilic  polymer matrix, such as in PVA and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) membranes, to introduce  CO2-reactive  effects  [216-218].  Mobile  carriers  are  often added to an FSC membrane for extra CO2 facilitated transport effect to reinforce the CO2 permeation properties [219-222]. Mobile carriers are found also increase gas diffusivities since these small molecules, typically organic molecules, usually plasticize the hosting polymer [12,223225]. It is worth mentioning that mobile carriers can also be attached to nanofillers embedded in polymeric membranes. By functionalizing the nanofillers, amine groups or other facilitated transport agents can be grafted onto the nanofillers, leading to improved CO2  separation performances [190,226].
texts.text: Small CO2-philic or reactive molecules ( Fig. 10 (b)) , including amino acid salts, ionic liquids, mimic enzymes, and KOH, have been used as mobile  carriers  and  added  into  a  non-reactive  hydrophilic  polymer matrix, such as in PVA and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) membranes, to introduce  CO2-reactive  effects  [216-218].  Mobile  carriers  are  often added to an FSC membrane for extra CO2 facilitated transport effect to reinforce the CO2 permeation properties [219-222]. Mobile carriers are found also increase gas diffusivities since these small molecules, typically organic molecules, usually plasticize the hosting polymer [12,223225]. It is worth mentioning that mobile carriers can also be attached to nanofillers embedded in polymeric membranes. By functionalizing the nanofillers, amine groups or other facilitated transport agents can be grafted onto the nanofillers, leading to improved CO2  separation performances [190,226].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A disadvantage of FTMs is the carrier saturation phenomenon at high CO2  partial  pressures,  which  will  lead  to  the  loss  of  the  facilitated transport effect. It is thus not the best option to use FTMs for separations at elevated pressures. However, it has been reported that the facilitated transport effect is positively influenced by increasing membrane thickness [12,227] or adding mobile carriers and nanofillers [199,228]; thus, CO2  separation  can  be  achieved  even  at  high  CO2  partial  pressures. Moreover, for post-combustion CO2 capture where the flue gas is at near ambient pressure with a CO2 concentration of around only 3% -15%, i.e., at a low CO2  partial pressure, FTMs could outperform other types of membranes [229].

It is thus not the best option to use FTMs for separations at elevated pressures. However, it has been reported that the facilitated transport effect is positively influenced by increasing membrane thickness [12,227] or adding mobile carriers and nanofillers [199,228]; thus, CO2  separation  can  be  achieved  even  at  high  CO2  partial  pressures. Moreover, for post-combustion CO2 capture where the flue gas is at near ambient pressure with a CO2 concentration of around only 3% -15%, i.e., at a low CO2  partial pressure, FTMs could outperform other types of membranes [229].

texts.text: :HSDUDWLRQ DQG kXULϧFDWLRQ LHFKQRORJ\ ssv iPePA  2PD Z. Dai and L. Deng

texts.label: text
texts.orig: temperature window for CO2 separation [230,231]. As porous inorganic membranes separate gases based mostly on molecule sieving, high CO2 selectivity may be achieved if the pore sizes of the membranes can be precisely tuned to be between the sizes of the gas pair, such as in zeolite [232], MOFs[233], and carbon molecular sieve (CMS) membranes. In particular,  CMS  membranes  have  attracted  special  attention  for  CO2 separation  [234-236].  CMS  membranes  are  typically  prepared  by carbonizing  polymeric  precursors  such  as  PI,  poly(phenylene  oxide) (PPO), and cellulose derivatives. CA-based hollow fiber carbon membranes  were  fabricated  successfully  and  tested  for  biogas  upgrading, natural gas sweetening, and H2 separation with excellent CO2 separation performance [234]. However, CMS membranes are somewhat brittle and often suffer from aging issues and pore blockage of water vapor in humid gas streams (RH > 30%). Module construction is also difficult due to the relatively brittle structures. Recently, Richter et al. [237] reported a high flux ceramic-supported carbon membrane with a CO2 permeance of 220 GPU and CO2/CH4  selectivity of 30, which may be used as the membrane for natural gas sweetening against high pressures. Dai et al. [238]  reported  a  CMS  using  Tr ¨ oger ' s  base  polymer  as  precursor, showing  excellent  CO2/H2  separation  performance.  New  CMS  membranes fabricated at a relatively low temperature that are more flexible and durable during module fabrication were also reported[238,239]. There have been several reports on zeolite membranes and MOF membranes in for CO2 separation based on molecular sieving [233,240-242], and  zeolite  membranes  with  high  CO2/CH4  separation  performances (CO2  permeance 1.2 × 10 7  mol m 2 Pa 1 s 1 ,  CO2/CH4  selectivity between 262 ~ 364, selective layer ~ 1 µ m) can be obtained by properly control and optimize the membrane fabrication parameters[243], but not  yet  received  industrial  interest.  Palladium  (Pd)-based  metallic membranes  have also been investigated for H2/CO2 separation [244,245]. The most attracting part of Pd membranes is the extremely high  H2 selectivity  as  the  result  of  the  proton  conductive  transport mechanism. The main bottlenecks hindering the industrial application of Pd membranes are its high price and poor poison resistance [246]. Researchers have also attempted to apply Pd-based metallic membranes as  membrane  reactors  for  H2 production  with  CO2  capture  for  CCS [247,248]. Attempts have been also made on adding Pd nanoparticles into  polymeric  matrix  to  improve  H2/CO2 separation  performances [249].
texts.text: temperature window for CO2 separation [230,231]. However, CMS membranes are somewhat brittle and often suffer from aging issues and pore blockage of water vapor in humid gas streams (RH > 30%). Module construction is also difficult due to the relatively brittle structures. Recently, Richter et al. [237] reported a high flux ceramic-supported carbon membrane with a CO2 permeance of 220 GPU and CO2/CH4  selectivity of 30, which may be used as the membrane for natural gas sweetening against high pressures. Dai et al. [238]  reported  a  CMS  using  Tr ¨ oger ' s  base  polymer  as  precursor, showing  excellent  CO2/H2  separation  performance.  New  CMS  membranes fabricated at a relatively low temperature that are more flexible and durable during module fabrication were also reported[238,239]. There have been several reports on zeolite membranes and MOF membranes in for CO2 separation based on molecular sieving [233,240-242], and  zeolite  membranes  with  high  CO2/CH4  separation  performances (CO2  permeance 1.2 × 10 7  mol m 2 Pa 1 s 1 ,  CO2/CH4  selectivity between 262 ~ 364, selective layer ~ 1 µ m) can be obtained by properly control and optimize the membrane fabrication parameters[243], but not  yet  received  industrial  interest.  Palladium  (Pd)-based  metallic membranes  have also been investigated for H2/CO2 separation [244,245]. The most attracting part of Pd membranes is the extremely high  H2 selectivity  as  the  result  of  the  proton  conductive  transport mechanism. The main bottlenecks hindering the industrial application of Pd membranes are its high price and poor poison resistance [246]. Researchers have also attempted to apply Pd-based metallic membranes as  membrane  reactors  for  H2 production  with  CO2  capture  for  CCS [247,248]. Attempts have been also made on adding Pd nanoparticles into  polymeric  matrix  to  improve  H2/CO2 separation  performances [249].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4. Hybrid membranes
texts.text: 3.4. Hybrid membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hybrid membranes, often referred to as mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) or nanocomposite membranes in the literature, can be fabricated by embedding various fillers in polymeric matrices [250]. MMMs consist of two materials governed by different transport mechanisms;
texts.text: Hybrid membranes, often referred to as mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) or nanocomposite membranes in the literature, can be fabricated by embedding various fillers in polymeric matrices [250]. MMMs consist of two materials governed by different transport mechanisms;

texts.label: text
texts.orig: thus,  such  membranes  have  the  potential  to  synergistically  combine superior gas-separation performance of the porous inorganic filler phase with the easy processability of polymers. Some recently reported porous filler are shown in Fig. 11 (a) [182,251].
texts.text: thus,  such  membranes  have  the  potential  to  synergistically  combine superior gas-separation performance of the porous inorganic filler phase with the easy processability of polymers. Some recently reported porous filler are shown in Fig. 11 (a) [182,251].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nanocomposite  membranes  are  usually  made  of  polymeric  membranes  with  dispersed  nano-sized  fillers.  Based  on  the  morphology, fillers can be nanoparticles (0D), e.g., fumed silica and TiO2; nanofibers (1D), such as CNT and nanocellulose, and nanosheets (2D), e.g., graphene, GO, covalent organic framework (COF) (Fig. 11 (b) ) [252], or 3D structured porous materials (MOF, Zeolites, porous aromatic framework (PAF), etc.) [253-256].
texts.text: Nanocomposite  membranes  are  usually  made  of  polymeric  membranes  with  dispersed  nano-sized  fillers.  Based  on  the  morphology, fillers can be nanoparticles (0D), e.g., fumed silica and TiO2; nanofibers (1D), such as CNT and nanocellulose, and nanosheets (2D), e.g., graphene, GO, covalent organic framework (COF) (Fig. 11 (b) ) [252], or 3D structured porous materials (MOF, Zeolites, porous aromatic framework (PAF), etc.) [253-256].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If  good  dispersion  and  interaction  of  the  fillers  with  the  polymer matrix are achieved (i.e., the ideal situation as shown in Fig. 11 (c) ), the presence of filler could significantly improve both CO2 permeability and CO2  selectivity of the hybrid membrane [201,258-260]. However, the performance may not be improved as expected if non-selective microvoids are formed between fillers and the polymeric phase (shown as Sieve-in-cage  in  Fig.  11 (c) );  there  will  only  be  an  increase  in  gas permeation  with  no  effect  on  selectivity  for  the  separation  [257]. Rigidified  interface  and  plugged  sieves  should  also  be  avoided.  It  is critical to make the fillers and polymer compatible. Different functionalization  methods  have  been  applied  to  achieve  ideal  dispersion [261,262]. In some cases, a third compound was introduced to make three-phase hybrid membranes, such as CO2-philic liquids (e.g., ILs), and  the  presence  of  the  third  compound  significantly  improves  the compatibility  between  fillers  and  the  polymer  phase  [88,263,264]. However, introducing a third compound may lead to other problems, like un-desired phase separation during membrane fabrication.
texts.text: If  good  dispersion  and  interaction  of  the  fillers  with  the  polymer matrix are achieved (i.e., the ideal situation as shown in Fig. 11 (c) ), the presence of filler could significantly improve both CO2 permeability and CO2  selectivity of the hybrid membrane [201,258-260]. However, the performance may not be improved as expected if non-selective microvoids are formed between fillers and the polymeric phase (shown as Sieve-in-cage  in  Fig.  11 (c) );  there  will  only  be  an  increase  in  gas permeation  with  no  effect  on  selectivity  for  the  separation  [257]. Rigidified  interface  and  plugged  sieves  should  also  be  avoided.  It  is critical to make the fillers and polymer compatible. Different functionalization  methods  have  been  applied  to  achieve  ideal  dispersion [261,262]. In some cases, a third compound was introduced to make three-phase hybrid membranes, such as CO2-philic liquids (e.g., ILs), and  the  presence  of  the  third  compound  significantly  improves  the compatibility  between  fillers  and  the  polymer  phase  [88,263,264]. However, introducing a third compound may lead to other problems, like un-desired phase separation during membrane fabrication.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most hybrid membranes fabricated in labs are self-standing  films with thicknesses in the range of tens of micrometer. Reducing membrane thickness to minimize the membrane ' s resistance can be the bottleneck for the commercialization of most hybrid membranes since the minimum membrane thickness of the membrane depends on and is often limited by the size of the fillers or their agglomerations [265]. In the past decades, lots of efforts have been dedicated to fabricating TFC membranes  with  a  hybrid  dense  coating  layer  [265].  Nanocellulose  and functionalized GO-based TFC membranes have been successfully fabricated and presented good CO2 separation performances [200,219,266]. Due to the intrinsic advantages of 2D nano sheets in fabricating TFC membranes, 2D materials have been widely used in hybrid membranes for  CO2 separation  [261,267,268].  Interestingly,  Zhu  et  al.  [269] developed  a  molecularly  mixed  composite  membrane  using  soluble porous organic cages as fillers with perfect dispersion, while Chen et al. [63] reported a solid-solvent-processing approach to coat ultrathin and highly loaded MMMs, leading to a significant increase in CO2  permeability and selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane long term stability and durability is always a concern for all the above mention membrane materials [270]. In the past few decades, many efforts have been devoted to investigate and improve the membrane durability  for  CO2  separation.  However,  many  tests  were carried out with binary or ternary simulated CO2 mixtures [271,272], in case of CO2  capture from flue gases, SOX  and NOx  are the most investigated  impurities  [273],  while  for  CO2/CH4  separation  applications, H2S is intensively investigated as impurities [272]. Those results could for sure offer info to predict the lifetime of the CO2  separation membranes.  However,  in  practical  applications,  impurities  are  normally much more complicated compared to the simulated binary or ternary mixtures made in the lab. Higher temperature and pressure fluctuations makes membrane-based CO2 practical applications much tougher than expected,  leading  to  faster  separation  performances  loss  and  shorter lifetime.
texts.text: Membrane long term stability and durability is always a concern for all the above mention membrane materials [270]. In the past few decades, many efforts have been devoted to investigate and improve the membrane durability  for  CO2  separation.  However,  many  tests  were carried out with binary or ternary simulated CO2 mixtures [271,272], in case of CO2  capture from flue gases, SOX  and NOx  are the most investigated  impurities  [273],  while  for  CO2/CH4  separation  applications, H2S is intensively investigated as impurities [272]. Those results could for sure offer info to predict the lifetime of the CO2  separation membranes.  However,  in  practical  applications,  impurities  are  normally much more complicated compared to the simulated binary or ternary mixtures made in the lab. Higher temperature and pressure fluctuations makes membrane-based CO2 practical applications much tougher than expected,  leading  to  faster  separation  performances  loss  and  shorter lifetime.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  addition,  for  practical  applications,  the  membrane  materials investigated above need to be fabricated into either asymmetric membrane with a selective skin layer orTFC membranes with a dense selective  layer  (normally < 1 μ m).  However,  for  many  of  the  abovementioned  membrane  materials,  some  of  them  may  be  difficult  to form asymmetric membranes via phase inversion technology [61,274], thus TFC membranes can be another option. Compared to asymmetric membranes which normally be fabricated via phase inversion technology, TFC membranes can be made by different coating methods (e.g., dip-coating, spin-coating and spry coating) or interfacial polymerization [8]. There have been many excellent reviews focusing on developing of TFC membranes for CO2 separation [65,66,80,275-277]. Generally, the choice of materials available to form TFC selective layer is much more than  fabricating  asymmetric  membranes.  However,  most  study  in membrane-based CO2  separation stopped at the stage of self-standing thick  films  with  thickness  of  over  50 μ m,  sometimes  even > 100 μ m. One possible reason is that it is relatively challenge to find proper porous support with sufficient molecule weight cut off, another possible reason can be that it is also challenge to find a proper coating technology with good  enough  reproducibility.  For  instance,  spin  coating  and  spray coating is considered to be relatively hard to reproduce and scale up. A third reason can be that finding a suitable solvent for the selective layer polymer, while not dissolving the porous supporting during the membrane fabrication  processes.  Possible  solutions  to  over  the  aforementioned problems can be tricky but surface modification, applying gutter layer and using a non-solvent liquid as pore forming agent can be a few strategies worth trying [219].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.5. Effect of support substrates in composite membranes
texts.text: 3.5. Effect of support substrates in composite membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: TFC membranes with a thin selective layer, preferentially < 1 µ m, are desired for practical gas separation applications. The porous substrate in a TFC membrane is used to provide mechanical strength and structural integrity while preventing pore-filling by the dope solution during coating [66]. To prevent pore filling during coating, it is also common to add a gutter layer before coating the selective layer. Based on the resistance-in-series  model,  it  is  well-accepted  that  the  permeability of the neat porous support should be at least 10 times higher than that of the resulting TFC membrane to ensure that over 90% of mass transfer resistance is generated from the selective layer [80]. Otherwise, the mass transfer resistance will gradually shift to the porous support, reducing the overall gas flux across the membrane [9].
texts.text: TFC membranes with a thin selective layer, preferentially < 1 µ m, are desired for practical gas separation applications. The porous substrate in a TFC membrane is used to provide mechanical strength and structural integrity while preventing pore-filling by the dope solution during coating [66]. To prevent pore filling during coating, it is also common to add a gutter layer before coating the selective layer. Based on the resistance-in-series  model,  it  is  well-accepted  that  the  permeability of the neat porous support should be at least 10 times higher than that of the resulting TFC membrane to ensure that over 90% of mass transfer resistance is generated from the selective layer [80]. Otherwise, the mass transfer resistance will gradually shift to the porous support, reducing the overall gas flux across the membrane [9].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The pore geometry, porosity, and surface pore size distribution can influence  the  overall  performance  of  TFC  membranes  [9,278].  It  is desired to have porous substrates with high permeance ( > 10 5 GPU) [9], smooth  surface  (to  reduce  roughness  and  ensure  ultrathin  selective layer), good compatibility with the selective layer materials, and good solvent resistance. In addition, the membrane pore structure, i.e., finger type or sponge type, is an important feature to consider when selecting membrane substrates. The finger-type porous support may easily deform under pressure, so a sponge-type structure is preferred for processes at elevated pressures. Polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF), PSf and PES are the most accessible and commonly used polymeric porous substrates [7,279], while porous ceramics or stainless-steel films are commonly seen as substrates used for inorganic TFC membranes [280].
texts.text: The pore geometry, porosity, and surface pore size distribution can influence  the  overall  performance  of  TFC  membranes  [9,278].  It  is desired to have porous substrates with high permeance ( > 10 5 GPU) [9], smooth  surface  (to  reduce  roughness  and  ensure  ultrathin  selective layer), good compatibility with the selective layer materials, and good solvent resistance. In addition, the membrane pore structure, i.e., finger type or sponge type, is an important feature to consider when selecting membrane substrates. The finger-type porous support may easily deform under pressure, so a sponge-type structure is preferred for processes at elevated pressures. Polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF), PSf and PES are the most accessible and commonly used polymeric porous substrates [7,279], while porous ceramics or stainless-steel films are commonly seen as substrates used for inorganic TFC membranes [280].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Please  note  that  we  have  only  discussed  membrane  materials attracted industrial interest to a certain extent. A more complete list of the most common membrane materials and their major applications in the literature can be found from several recent review articles on CO2 separation membranes [7,11,12,19,39,65,213,232,281-286].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Membrane module configurations
texts.text: 4. Membrane module configurations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membranes must be installed in modules to separate and leak-tight the feed side and permeate side streams to achieve the separation. A membrane module is also expected to protect membranes against mechanical damage and provide high productivity of the process, e.g., with a high ratio of membrane surface to module volume, often referred to as packing density. The main features of different membrane modules are summarized in Table SI-2 [287], including plate-and-frame and spiral wound modules of flat sheet membranes and hollow fiber and tubular modules of tubular-shaped membranes.
texts.text: Membranes must be installed in modules to separate and leak-tight the feed side and permeate side streams to achieve the separation. A membrane module is also expected to protect membranes against mechanical damage and provide high productivity of the process, e.g., with a high ratio of membrane surface to module volume, often referred to as packing density. The main features of different membrane modules are summarized in Table SI-2 [287], including plate-and-frame and spiral wound modules of flat sheet membranes and hollow fiber and tubular modules of tubular-shaped membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig.  12  presents  the  structures  of  the  three  most  applied  module configurations for CO2  separation. Spiral wound modules (Fig. 12 (a)) are the most commonly used configuration for flat sheet membranes due to  their  higher  packing density than plate-and-frame modules. Spiral wound modules  have  adjustable  hydrodynamics,  and  optimizing  the feed spacer can reduce boundary layers along the membranes and lessen the potential concentration polarization effect. Hollow fiber modules (Fig. 12 (b)) has the highest packing density of all configurations hence the lowest relative costs. Generally, it is easier to get higher (3 -4 times) packing density in hollow fiber membrane modules than in spiral wound modules [290]. As hollow fiber membranes maximize the surface area per  unit  volume,  they  are  particularly  suitable  for  processing  largevolume gas flows. Due to low packing densities, plate-and-frame configurations are found to be used only in a few pilot tests, while tubular membrane modules are only employed when the membrane materials are more suitable to be made into tubular forms, such as metallic or ceramic membranes, as seen in Fig. 12 (c) .
texts.text: Fig.  12  presents  the  structures  of  the  three  most  applied  module configurations for CO2  separation. Spiral wound modules (Fig. 12 (a)) are the most commonly used configuration for flat sheet membranes due to  their  higher  packing density than plate-and-frame modules. Spiral wound modules  have  adjustable  hydrodynamics,  and  optimizing  the feed spacer can reduce boundary layers along the membranes and lessen the potential concentration polarization effect. Hollow fiber modules (Fig. 12 (b)) has the highest packing density of all configurations hence the lowest relative costs. Generally, it is easier to get higher (3 -4 times) packing density in hollow fiber membrane modules than in spiral wound modules [290]. As hollow fiber membranes maximize the surface area per  unit  volume,  they  are  particularly  suitable  for  processing  largevolume gas flows. Due to low packing densities, plate-and-frame configurations are found to be used only in a few pilot tests, while tubular membrane modules are only employed when the membrane materials are more suitable to be made into tubular forms, such as metallic or ceramic membranes, as seen in Fig. 12 (c) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane separation performance is found to depend significantly on module configurations [291]. Besides the influences from packing density,  flow  patterns  in  different  module  configurations  affect  the concentration  profile,  boundary  layer,  pressure  drop,  and  the  consequent mass transfer efficiency in membrane modules and, eventually, the  separation  performance  [292].  Gas  transport  in  modules  shows different  flow  patterns  in  different  module  configurations.  Typically, cross-flow is the flow pattern in spiral wound membrane modules, and counter-current flow often applies in hollow fiber membrane modules [292].

Besides the influences from packing density,  flow  patterns  in  different  module  configurations  affect  the concentration  profile,  boundary  layer,  pressure  drop,  and  the  consequent mass transfer efficiency in membrane modules and, eventually, the  separation  performance  [292].  Gas  transport  in  modules  shows different  flow  patterns  in  different  module  configurations.  Typically, cross-flow is the flow pattern in spiral wound membrane modules, and counter-current flow often applies in hollow fiber membrane modules [292].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When  highly  CO2-permeable  membranes  are  used,  concentration polarization may occur, which may cause a decrease in the driving force and serious pressure drop along the membrane, undermining the module performance. Although counter-current flow gives a higher average driving force than cross flow for the same separation conditions [290], the  better  hydrodynamics  in  spiral  wound  modules  makes  them  less suffered from concentration polarization issues. Nonetheless, the flow pattern  in  hollow  fiber  membranes  can  be  optimized  to  reduce  concentration polarization effects, such as using tangential flow directions for feed gas inlet or installing a central tube as the retentate gas outlet to change  flow  directions ( Fig.  12 (b) ).  It  may  be  beneficial  to  take  a double-ended flow as the permeate outlet or, sometimes, use a relatively lower packing density.

texts.text: Fig.  12. Sample  spiral  wound  membrane  module  (a),  hollow  fiber  membrane  module  with  a  central  pipe  (b),  and  Pd-based  tubular  membrane  module  (c). Reproduced from ref [31,288,289].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: solution-diffusion mechanism are also greatly influenced by RH, e.g., Nafion [114], Pebax [201,294] and Nexar [295] membranes. Therefore, it is of critical importance to ensure sufficient RH inside the modules to maintain the overall module performances. Yang et al. [296,297] reported a tanks-in-series approach in a modeling study for mass transfer in amine-based FTMs to predict the variations of water vapor contents, temperature, and pressures along the membrane module, as shown in Fig. 13. Such models can be integrated into commercial software (e.g., Aspen HYSYS) for up-scaling membranes or more accurate performance estimation, particularly for FT membrane systems. In addition, the gas flow along membranes (e.g., along the length of hollow fibers) can be optimized according to the CO2  concentration profile to avoid reverse transport and maximize the driving force. With a proper design of hollow fiber membrane module configurations, the mass transfer coefficient may become higher than conventional spiral wound membrane modules [298]. Therefore, optimized hollow fiber membrane modules can be the most promising configuration in gas separation applications [299].

With a proper design of hollow fiber membrane module configurations, the mass transfer coefficient may become higher than conventional spiral wound membrane modules [298]. Therefore, optimized hollow fiber membrane modules can be the most promising configuration in gas separation applications [299].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pressure drop and leaking, especially at elevated temperatures and pressures, are also issues related to membrane modules. Pressure loss partly comes from the flow friction through the flow channels along the fiber or between the narrow spaces of flat sheet envelopes. Another part of the pressure drop is due to the loss of the permeated gas. As a result, the transmembrane pressure decreases along the membrane, reducing driving force and hence the overall performance. Hollow fiber membranes  with  the  counter-current  flow  will  suffer  the  most  with  the pressure drop. A smart design is to change the flow pattern to cross-flow, such as by introducing a central pipe for the feed outlet and forcing the flow direction of the feed (shell side) and permeate (the tube side) cross (Fig.  12 (b) ).  However,  with  the  shortened  flow  path,  the  stage-cut

texts.orig: It is worth noting that, as CO2  has a very high Joule-Thomson coefficient  (1.11  K/bar),  the  Joule -Thomson effect  takes  place  in  CO2 separation  membranes  and  makes  notable  influence  in  temperature when the pressure drop across membranes is high [50]. Gas tends to expand from the high pressure side to the low pressure side, resulting in temperature drop under constant enthalpy [300,301]. Thus, the temperature influence must be considered when the CO2  permeation in a membrane is sensitive to temperature [302].

Thus, the temperature influence must be considered when the CO2  permeation in a membrane is sensitive to temperature [302].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Process design considerations
texts.text: 5. Process design considerations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A techno-economic feasibility study is always the first investigation before any new membrane can be applied in an industrial separation process, and process design is the basis for such a study. As the outcome, an optimized process design will be determined as the foundation of a new membrane process. Needless to say, CO2 separation performance of the membrane plays the most critical role in the design of the membrane system. Based on the selected membrane material and its performance, considering the process conditions (e.g., P, T, feed CO2  concentration and  flow  rate)  and  product  requirements  (purity  and  recovery),  the design  of  a  membrane  system  starts  from  determining  the  module configuration, stage design (e.g., single- or multi- stage), stage layout, and the option of internal recycling streams [49,303]. For instance, in CO2  capture processes, one-stage membrane separation is not usually sufficient to offer the desired CO2  purity if the feed gas CO2  concentration is low or the required product purity is too high. Thus, two-stage membrane processes, and if necessary, three or even four-stage membrane processes, should be designed, but two-stage membrane processes

For a multi-stage process, a cascade solution is typically used [304], where part of the flow is recycled to achieve the recovery rate or CO2  purity in the final product stream. Two-stage membrane processes  of  two  different  cascade  solutions  and  recycle  streams  are shown in Fig. 14 [48]. texts.orig: A general trend for the costs (per ton of CO2 avoided) of various plant capacities,  from  small  to  large  scale,  is  drawn  based  on  the  process simulation results in the literature[305,307-313], showing the impact of plant  capacity  on  CO2 capture  costs  of  the  membrane-based  and absorption-based processes, as presented in Fig. 15. There exists a critical  turn  point  when  the  trends  presenting  two  technologies  cross, indicating that when the plant size is below the critical capacity, i.e., a small to medium scale, the total cost using the membrane process becomes  lower  than  that  in  an  absorption  process  for  per  ton  of  CO2 captured.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Industrial applications
texts.text: 6. Industrial applications

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane separation has been applied in industrial gas separation since the 1970 s, such as to enrich oxygen, H2  separation in ammonia plants,  and  CO2  removal  from  natural  gas,  but  the  market  share  for membranes has always been small. No application is there yet when CO2 capture from flue gas is considered. However, especially in recent years, significant opportunities have been identified for CO2 separation, thanks to the increased industrial acceptance of membrane technology and the demands  for  CCS  actions.  Membranes ' unique  advantages,  such  as compact system, low energy consumption, simple operation, and high modularity,  leading  to  enormous  technical,  environmental,  and  economic  benefits.  The  emerging  membrane  materials  and  fabrication techniques make membrane processes more competitive compared with other technologies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the past few decades, despite thousands of different membrane materials reported in the literature with CO2 separation performance far above the Robeson upper bounds, sadly, only a few membrane materials have been applied in industrial processes or being developed towards industrial  applications  (e.g.,  performed  pilot  tests).  Even  for  some membrane materials reported with superior separation performances, the costly synthesize route or challenges in fabricating lab-scale membranes into large scale or as asymmetric or TFC membrane forms may hinder  their  further  development  towards  industrial  applications. texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, commercial membranes or membranes that undergo pilot studies are presented in the context of industrial CO2  capture or separation processes. According to the key components of the separation, CO2 separation applications can be grouped into three major types, namely CO2/CH4  separation, CO2/N2  separation, and CO2/H2  separation. Since industrial processes for CO2  separation have very different process  conditions,  the  requirements  for  membranes  or  issues  to  be considered in different applications vary significantly.
texts.text: In this section, commercial membranes or membranes that undergo pilot studies are presented in the context of industrial CO2  capture or separation processes. According to the key components of the separation, CO2 separation applications can be grouped into three major types, namely CO2/CH4  separation, CO2/N2  separation, and CO2/H2  separation. Since industrial processes for CO2  separation have very different process  conditions,  the  requirements  for  membranes  or  issues  to  be considered in different applications vary significantly.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.1. CO2/CH4 separation
texts.text: 6.1. CO2/CH4 separation

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.1.1. Natural gas sweetening
texts.text: 6.1.1. Natural gas sweetening

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Natural gas sweetening, or acid gas removal from natural gas, is by far the largest industrial application in membrane-based CO2 separation, although membrane occupies only approximately 5% of the acid gas removal market where amine absorption technology has been dominating. Current key players in the CO2  separation membrane market include  Air  Products,  Honeywell  UOP,  Schlumberger,  and  MTR,  Inc. (US), Air Liquide (France), Ube Industries (Japan) [41].
texts.text: Natural gas sweetening, or acid gas removal from natural gas, is by far the largest industrial application in membrane-based CO2 separation, although membrane occupies only approximately 5% of the acid gas removal market where amine absorption technology has been dominating. Current key players in the CO2  separation membrane market include  Air  Products,  Honeywell  UOP,  Schlumberger,  and  MTR,  Inc. (US), Air Liquide (France), Ube Industries (Japan) [41].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As  one  of  the  earliest  commercial  membrane  materials  since  the 1950 s, cellulose acetate (CA) is still the most commonly used polymeric membrane today and is widely used for natural gas sweetening in industrial processes. Compared to the up-to-date gas permeation date in the literature, CA membranes display very low CO2 permeability (~10 Barrer) and CO2/CH4 selectivity (~40 for single gas tests and ~ 20 for mixed  gas  tests)  [314].  Matrimid  is  another  commercial  membrane material for CO2/CH4  separation, presenting a CO2/CH4  selectivity of between 30 and 60 [315]. PSf membranes have also been applied for CO2/CH4 separation, demonstrating permeability values of 20 -40 Barrer and CO2/CH4 selectivity of 15 -35 [316]. It is worth mentioning that different producers may fabricated them into CO2 separation membrane modules with different selective layer thickness, thus leading to variations in gas permeances even the membrane modules were prepared via the same material. Even though these membrane materials exhibited relatively  low  CO2 separation  performances,  their  performances  are reasonably stable over time, and to a certain extent, they show plasticization resistance. Since these membrane materials are available at low cost,  their  market  shares  in  natural  gas  sweetening  applications  are assured. Information on the current commercially available gas separation membranes for natural gas sweetening applications is summarized in Table SI-3 [317,318].
texts.text: As  one  of  the  earliest  commercial  membrane  materials  since  the 1950 s, cellulose acetate (CA) is still the most commonly used polymeric membrane today and is widely used for natural gas sweetening in industrial processes. Compared to the up-to-date gas permeation date in the literature, CA membranes display very low CO2 permeability (~10 Barrer) and CO2/CH4 selectivity (~40 for single gas tests and ~ 20 for mixed  gas  tests)  [314].  Matrimid  is  another  commercial  membrane material for CO2/CH4  separation, presenting a CO2/CH4  selectivity of between 30 and 60 [315]. PSf membranes have also been applied for CO2/CH4 separation, demonstrating permeability values of 20 -40 Barrer and CO2/CH4 selectivity of 15 -35 [316]. It is worth mentioning that different producers may fabricated them into CO2 separation membrane modules with different selective layer thickness, thus leading to variations in gas permeances even the membrane modules were prepared via the same material. Even though these membrane materials exhibited relatively  low  CO2 separation  performances,  their  performances  are reasonably stable over time, and to a certain extent, they show plasticization resistance. Since these membrane materials are available at low cost,  their  market  shares  in  natural  gas  sweetening  applications  are assured. Information on the current commercially available gas separation membranes for natural gas sweetening applications is summarized in Table SI-3 [317,318].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Up to now, more than 20 large CO2 membrane removal plants have been built over the world [319], such as the Cynara/Cameron plant in South East Asia and the Separex membranes in Spain (using UOP technology). A few smaller systems have also been developed. For instance, for offshore CO2 separation systems, membrane technology has become a  favorite  choice  due  to  the  demands  for  small  footprints  and  high environmental standards [320].
texts.text: Up to now, more than 20 large CO2 membrane removal plants have been built over the world [319], such as the Cynara/Cameron plant in South East Asia and the Separex membranes in Spain (using UOP technology). A few smaller systems have also been developed. For instance, for offshore CO2 separation systems, membrane technology has become a  favorite  choice  due  to  the  demands  for  small  footprints  and  high environmental standards [320].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The problems of plasticization in this process are particularly severe because that natural gas sweetening is carried out under high pressures and both CO2  and CH4  cause plasticization, especially in the thin selective layers, resulting in less selective separation and hence more CH4 loss from the process. Thus, the reduction of membrane selectivity must be considered when designing a process with potential plasticization issues. In the past few decades, abundant efforts have been dedicated to develop  new  strategies  to  improve  the  membrane  anti-plasticization resistance,  including  cross-linking  (e.g.,  chemical  cross  linking[321] and  thermal  crosslinking[322,323]),  thermal  treatment  [324,325], adding nanofillers [326,327] and polymer blending [328]. With proper treatment, some of the membranes demonstrated much better plasticization resistance even under feed pressure up to 30 ~ 50 Bars [329,330]. There have been many excellent reviews focusing on this topic available [318,331,332].

Thus, the reduction of membrane selectivity must be considered when designing a process with potential plasticization issues. With proper treatment, some of the membranes demonstrated much better plasticization resistance even under feed pressure up to 30 ~ 50 Bars [329,330]. There have been many excellent reviews focusing on this topic available [318,331,332].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.1.2. Biogas upgrading
texts.text: 6.1.2. Biogas upgrading

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Biogas is a promising renewable fuel after being upgraded to biomethane. Compared to natural gas, biogas has much higher CO2  content in the feed, and the production capacity of biogas plants is much smaller. Due to the high CO2 concentration and small to medium plant size, membrane separation is a comparatively cheap process with lower operating and capital costs and simpler and more compact membrane modules than other CO2 separation technologies [333], as indicated in Fig. 16 (a) and (b) . The energy demand is also much less for the membrane process.
texts.text: Biogas is a promising renewable fuel after being upgraded to biomethane. Compared to natural gas, biogas has much higher CO2  content in the feed, and the production capacity of biogas plants is much smaller. Due to the high CO2 concentration and small to medium plant size, membrane separation is a comparatively cheap process with lower operating and capital costs and simpler and more compact membrane modules than other CO2 separation technologies [333], as indicated in Fig. 16 (a) and (b) . The energy demand is also much less for the membrane process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  large  number  of  membrane  materials  have  been  reported  for biogas upgrading, but commercial polymeric membranes for this process are still limited to CA, PSf, PEI, and PI, similar to membranes used for  natural  gas  sweetening.  Inorganic  membranes,  including  carbon molecular sieves, zeolites, and silica membranes, were also reported for biogas upgrading applications, but no industrial application yet [335]. CMS hollow fiber membrane modules of a 2 m 2 area were made to test the membrane performance at the pilot plant with a capacity of processing 20 Nm 3 /h of biogas [336]. Compared to PI membranes with relatively  higher  CO2 permeance  (~20  GPU)  and  lower  CO2/CH4 selectivity (~33), CMS membranes with lower CO2  permeance (7.75) and higher CO2/CH4 selectivity (246) shows better energy efficiency as less membrane stage was needed to reach the purification target.
texts.text: A  large  number  of  membrane  materials  have  been  reported  for biogas upgrading, but commercial polymeric membranes for this process are still limited to CA, PSf, PEI, and PI, similar to membranes used for  natural  gas  sweetening.  Inorganic  membranes,  including  carbon molecular sieves, zeolites, and silica membranes, were also reported for biogas upgrading applications, but no industrial application yet [335]. CMS hollow fiber membrane modules of a 2 m 2 area were made to test the membrane performance at the pilot plant with a capacity of processing 20 Nm 3 /h of biogas [336]. Compared to PI membranes with relatively  higher  CO2 permeance  (~20  GPU)  and  lower  CO2/CH4 selectivity (~33), CMS membranes with lower CO2  permeance (7.75) and higher CO2/CH4 selectivity (246) shows better energy efficiency as less membrane stage was needed to reach the purification target.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to the limitation of either permeance and/or selectivity, multistage processes, 2-stage in most cases, are employed in membrane-based biogas  upgrading  processes  to  ensure  high  purity  or  low  CH4  losses [336] with one or both stages operate under elevated pressures. As raw biogas is produced at nearly ambient pressures, compressors become the major cost items of the biogas plants [307,335,336].
texts.text: Due to the limitation of either permeance and/or selectivity, multistage processes, 2-stage in most cases, are employed in membrane-based biogas  upgrading  processes  to  ensure  high  purity  or  low  CH4  losses [336] with one or both stages operate under elevated pressures. As raw biogas is produced at nearly ambient pressures, compressors become the major cost items of the biogas plants [307,335,336].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.2. CO2/N2 separation
texts.text: 6.2. CO2/N2 separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The process conditions of post-combustion flue gas, i.e., low CO2 partial  pressure,  large  and  fully  water-saturated  flow,  make  it  challenging  for  membrane  separation.  According  to  the  literature[337], membranes with CO2 permeance higher than 500 GPU and selectivity more than 40 may make membrane separation competitive with MEA absorption for CO2 capture. Several other reports agree with this statement [338,339] although they suggested different performance data as the threshold.
texts.text: The process conditions of post-combustion flue gas, i.e., low CO2 partial  pressure,  large  and  fully  water-saturated  flow,  make  it  challenging  for  membrane  separation.  According  to  the  literature[337], membranes with CO2 permeance higher than 500 GPU and selectivity more than 40 may make membrane separation competitive with MEA absorption for CO2 capture. Several other reports agree with this statement [338,339] although they suggested different performance data as the threshold.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Facilitated transport membranes have been successful in the development to pilot and demonstration scales. One example is the FTMs developed  by  the  Norwegian  University  of  Science  and  Technology (NTNU). Since 2011, pilot or pre-pilot tests based on FTM membranes have been carried out in several testing sites by NTNU with industrial partners (Air Products, etc.), including the Sines bituminous coal power station  (Sines,  Portugal)  [82],  the  Tiller  plant  (Trondheim,  Norway) [341],  the  Norcem  cement  factory  (Brevik,  Norway)  [342],  and  the Colacem cement plant (Gubbio, Italy) [76,343]. Flat sheet membrane modules were initially developed, but considering the large volume of flue gas, NTNU later changed its focus to hollow fiber membranes for higher packing density, as shown in Fig. 17 a . The fabricated membrane demonstrated good long-term stability over a testing period of up to 6 months [82]. The membrane also demonstrated excellent impurities resistance properties (e.g., SOx and NOx) [76]. NTNU ' s 2nd generation FTM membrane is under commercialization by Aqualung Carbon capture AS (Norway) [344].
texts.text: Facilitated transport membranes have been successful in the development to pilot and demonstration scales. One example is the FTMs developed  by  the  Norwegian  University  of  Science  and  Technology (NTNU). Since 2011, pilot or pre-pilot tests based on FTM membranes have been carried out in several testing sites by NTNU with industrial partners (Air Products, etc.), including the Sines bituminous coal power station  (Sines,  Portugal)  [82],  the  Tiller  plant  (Trondheim,  Norway) [341],  the  Norcem  cement  factory  (Brevik,  Norway)  [342],  and  the Colacem cement plant (Gubbio, Italy) [76,343]. Flat sheet membrane modules were initially developed, but considering the large volume of flue gas, NTNU later changed its focus to hollow fiber membranes for higher packing density, as shown in Fig. 17 a . The fabricated membrane demonstrated good long-term stability over a testing period of up to 6 months [82]. The membrane also demonstrated excellent impurities resistance properties (e.g., SOx and NOx) [76]. NTNU ' s 2nd generation FTM membrane is under commercialization by Aqualung Carbon capture AS (Norway) [344].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ohio state university (OSU) reported another effort of the field test for  their  FTMs  containing  both  mobile  and  fixed-site  amine  carriers [345,346] in spiral wound module configurations (Fig. 17 c ). The CO2 permeance  of  1450  GPU  with  CO2/N2  selectivity  of  185  was  documented at 67 ◦ C, and the module was demonstrated stable in a 500-h test. Tianjin University has also carried out pilot-scale projects of FTM membranes on CO2 capture from flue gas. A membrane production facility with a productivity of 11000 m 2 /y was established [347,348]. A 3stage membrane process using spiral wound modules was designed and installed for a CO2 capture capacity of 50,000 Nm 3 /y. A CO2 purity of up to 96.2% and a capture ratio of up to 81.3% were reported [347].
texts.text: Ohio state university (OSU) reported another effort of the field test for  their  FTMs  containing  both  mobile  and  fixed-site  amine  carriers [345,346] in spiral wound module configurations (Fig. 17 c ). The CO2 permeance  of  1450  GPU  with  CO2/N2  selectivity  of  185  was  documented at 67 ◦ C, and the module was demonstrated stable in a 500-h test. Tianjin University has also carried out pilot-scale projects of FTM membranes on CO2 capture from flue gas. A membrane production facility with a productivity of 11000 m 2 /y was established [347,348]. A 3stage membrane process using spiral wound modules was designed and installed for a CO2 capture capacity of 50,000 Nm 3 /y. A CO2 purity of up to 96.2% and a capture ratio of up to 81.3% were reported [347].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is worth mentioning that, since FTMs are sensitive to CO2 partial pressure in the feed, it is beneficial for the post-combustion flue gas conditions, i.e., low CO2 concentration at near ambient pressure with a large volume [304]. As water vapor is involved in the facilitated transport mechanism, FTM membranes are also considered favorable for industrial applications because the pre-treatment step for water removal may be avoided. Nevertheless, no FTMs have yet been fully commercialized, mostly due to the remaining gap between pilot and industrial scale FTM membrane modules for high packing density and the issues in water management inside the FTM modules for optimal water profile to ensure sufficient facilitated transport effects.

As water vapor is involved in the facilitated transport mechanism, FTM membranes are also considered favorable for industrial applications because the pre-treatment step for water removal may be avoided. Nevertheless, no FTMs have yet been fully commercialized, mostly due to the remaining gap between pilot and industrial scale FTM membrane modules for high packing density and the issues in water management inside the FTM modules for optimal water profile to ensure sufficient facilitated transport effects.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the past decades, great efforts have been dedicated to developing membranes  for  CO2/N2 separation  with  CO2/N2 separation  performances above the Robeson upper bounds. However, although numerous materials  have  been  developed,  only  a  small  number  of  them  were fabricated into TFC membranes with viable CO2  flux [80], and even fewer were successfully developed to pilot scale [340].
texts.text: In the past decades, great efforts have been dedicated to developing membranes  for  CO2/N2 separation  with  CO2/N2 separation  performances above the Robeson upper bounds. However, although numerous materials  have  been  developed,  only  a  small  number  of  them  were fabricated into TFC membranes with viable CO2  flux [80], and even fewer were successfully developed to pilot scale [340].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Choi and coworkers have also performed a pilot test using hollow fiber membrane modules for flue gas CO2  capture [351] by a 4-stage membrane process, in which CO2  purity of 99.2% with a recovery of 91.5% was achieved using a polyethersulfone (PES) membrane with CO2 permeance of only 60 GPU [351].
texts.text: Choi and coworkers have also performed a pilot test using hollow fiber membrane modules for flue gas CO2  capture [351] by a 4-stage membrane process, in which CO2  purity of 99.2% with a recovery of 91.5% was achieved using a polyethersulfone (PES) membrane with CO2 permeance of only 60 GPU [351].

texts.orig: MTR is  one  of  the  pioneer  companies  in  CO2  capture  pilot  tests [338,339]. Their first-generation Polaris membranes demonstrate CO2 permeance of ~ 1000 GPU, while its second generation shows a doubled CO2 permeance (~2000 GPU) with similar CO2/N2 selectivity (~50). In 2011, the 1st generation Polaris membrane as spiral wound modules was applied in a flue gas CO2 capture pilot test with a scale of 1 ton of CO2 per day, and later in 2015, it was extended to 20 tons of CO2  per day [339]. This membrane presented good stability over 1000 h stability tests.
texts.text: MTR is  one  of  the  pioneer  companies  in  CO2  capture  pilot  tests [338,339]. Their first-generation Polaris membranes demonstrate CO2 permeance of ~ 1000 GPU, while its second generation shows a doubled CO2 permeance (~2000 GPU) with similar CO2/N2 selectivity (~50). In 2011, the 1st generation Polaris membrane as spiral wound modules was applied in a flue gas CO2 capture pilot test with a scale of 1 ton of CO2 per day, and later in 2015, it was extended to 20 tons of CO2  per day [339]. This membrane presented good stability over 1000 h stability tests.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The reported pilot scale membranes and their performances can be found in Table SI-4 .  Some new polymeric membranes in the market with  the  potential  for  industrial  CO2 capture  processes  are  listed  in Table SI-5 [337]. Overall, most of the tested membranes in the pilot test scale show reasonable stability and performance over the tested period. However, it is worth noting that for many of the CO2/N2  separation membranes, decline of CO2  separation performances over time can be observed in pilot test conditions, as in practical flue gases, there are many different kinds of impurities, that are either difficult to investigate in lab tests (e.g., particulate matters), or considered not that important (e.g., O2) but actually has significant negative impact on the CO2 separation performances, especially for FT membranes with active amine carriers. In some cases, the water vapor in the flue gas could also seriously reduce the CO2 permeance over time, as the water vapor condense in the porous support and sharply increase the CO2 transport resistance across  the  membrane.  Developing  membrane  materials  with  better chemical and mechanical stability can be one option to overcome these challenges, another possibility is to add stricter pre-treatment before the membrane gas separation modules.

In some cases, the water vapor in the flue gas could also seriously reduce the CO2 permeance over time, as the water vapor condense in the porous support and sharply increase the CO2 transport resistance across  the  membrane.  Developing  membrane  materials  with  better chemical and mechanical stability can be one option to overcome these challenges, another possibility is to add stricter pre-treatment before the membrane gas separation modules.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.3. CO2/H2 separation
texts.text: 6.3. CO2/H2 separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2/H2 separation is in most cases for pre-combustion CO2 capture from syngas. Recently, CO2/H2 separation for bio-H2 production has also been reported. Due to the significant differences in the properties of CO2 and H2, i.e., H2 as a very small molecule has very high diffusivity while CO2 is a highly condensable gas with very high solubility, the CO2/H2 separation through a membrane may be either H2-selective [354,355] or CO2 selective [133], depending on the membranes ' intrinsic properties. For  H2 selective  membranes,  high  operation  temperature  is  more favorable for higher H2 diffusion that is enhanced at high temperatures [354,356,357],  while  for  CO2 selective  membranes,  low  operation temperature is preferred as CO2  solubility is higher at cold conditions except  for  FTM  membranes;  FTM  membranes show  excellent  performances even at elevated temperatures [228358].
texts.text: CO2/H2 separation is in most cases for pre-combustion CO2 capture from syngas. Recently, CO2/H2 separation for bio-H2 production has also been reported. Due to the significant differences in the properties of CO2 and H2, i.e., H2 as a very small molecule has very high diffusivity while CO2 is a highly condensable gas with very high solubility, the CO2/H2 separation through a membrane may be either H2-selective [354,355] or CO2 selective [133], depending on the membranes ' intrinsic properties. For  H2 selective  membranes,  high  operation  temperature  is  more favorable for higher H2 diffusion that is enhanced at high temperatures [354,356,357],  while  for  CO2 selective  membranes,  low  operation temperature is preferred as CO2  solubility is higher at cold conditions except  for  FTM  membranes;  FTM  membranes show  excellent  performances even at elevated temperatures [228358].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.3.1. CO2-selective membranes
texts.text: 6.3.1. CO2-selective membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2-selective  CO2/H2 separation  membranes  are  mostly  dense polymeric  membranes  showing  high  CO2-philicity  or  CO2-reactivity. PEO-based membranes are representative CO2-selective membranes for CO2/H2 separation. CO2-philic membranes without facilitated transport effect normally show low CO2/H2 selectivity ( < 30) [12]. Scholes et al. carried out CO2/H2 separation under pilot test conditions but with a labscale  membrane  area  [359].  All  the  tested  PDMS,  Pebax,  and  crosslinked  PEG  membranes  presented  relatively  low  CO2/H2  separation performances under pre-combustion conditions.
texts.text: CO2-selective  CO2/H2 separation  membranes  are  mostly  dense polymeric  membranes  showing  high  CO2-philicity  or  CO2-reactivity. PEO-based membranes are representative CO2-selective membranes for CO2/H2 separation. CO2-philic membranes without facilitated transport effect normally show low CO2/H2 selectivity ( < 30) [12]. Scholes et al. carried out CO2/H2 separation under pilot test conditions but with a labscale  membrane  area  [359].  All  the  tested  PDMS,  Pebax,  and  crosslinked  PEG  membranes  presented  relatively  low  CO2/H2  separation performances under pre-combustion conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, if proper facilitated transport agents can be introduced into the membrane, high CO2/H2 may be achieved because that syngas is saturated with water vapor and the presence of water vapor is beneficial for  CO2  facilitated  transport  [75,360].  For  instance,  by  employing  a sterically hindered amine membrane with facilitated transport carriers, Ho and coworkers reported a CO2/H2 selectivity of up to 300 with a CO2 permeability of over 3000 Barrer [75]. With the same concept, FTMs with mobile carriers also present promising CO2/H2 selectivity [191].

With the same concept, FTMs with mobile carriers also present promising CO2/H2 selectivity [191].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition, a ceramic-carbonate dual-phase membrane consisting of a molten carbonate phase and metal or ceramic phase can be an interesting option for high-temperature CO2  separation [361]. A high CO2 flux with reasonably stable performance was obtained from dual-phase hollow fiber membranes [362].

A high CO2 flux with reasonably stable performance was obtained from dual-phase hollow fiber membranes [362].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: membranes  for  H2 recovery  in  ammonia  plants  [363].  However, obtaining high H2/CO2  selectivity is challenging due to the high CO2 solubility  in  almost  all  polymers  [364].  Glassy  polymers  with  sizesieving ability generally exhibit H2  selectivity over CO2, such as highfree-volume, shape-persisting  polymers. Polybenzimidazole  (PBI) is a representative H2  selective membrane material for H2/CO2  separation [365],  which  prefers  higher  operation  temperature  that  favors  H2 diffusion but reduces CO2 sorption, significantly improving the H2/CO2 selectivity  [354].  In  addition,  embedding  metal  particles/wires  with high H2  solubility as nano additives to make hybrid membranes could promote H2 transport through the membranes [366,367]. O ' Brien et al. [368] investigated the possibility of  applying  PBI-based hollow fiber membranes for H2/CO2 separation. The PBI membrane exhibited an H2/ CO2 selectivity of over 40 and permeability of over 10 Barrer at 250 ◦ C. A pilot-scale separation system of this membrane was designed, but no experimental results were reported on this work.
texts.text: membranes  for  H2 recovery  in  ammonia  plants  [363].  However, obtaining high H2/CO2  selectivity is challenging due to the high CO2 solubility  in  almost  all  polymers  [364].  Glassy  polymers  with  sizesieving ability generally exhibit H2  selectivity over CO2, such as highfree-volume, shape-persisting  polymers. Polybenzimidazole  (PBI) is a representative H2  selective membrane material for H2/CO2  separation [365],  which  prefers  higher  operation  temperature  that  favors  H2 diffusion but reduces CO2 sorption, significantly improving the H2/CO2 selectivity  [354].  In  addition,  embedding  metal  particles/wires  with high H2  solubility as nano additives to make hybrid membranes could promote H2 transport through the membranes [366,367]. O ' Brien et al. [368] investigated the possibility of  applying  PBI-based hollow fiber membranes for H2/CO2 separation. The PBI membrane exhibited an H2/ CO2 selectivity of over 40 and permeability of over 10 Barrer at 250 ◦ C. A pilot-scale separation system of this membrane was designed, but no experimental results were reported on this work.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Inorganic  membranes  such  as  metallic  membranes,  microporous ceramic membranes, carbon molecular sieving membranes, and dense ceramic  membranes  have  also  been  studied  for  H2/CO2 separation [369]. Compared to polymeric membranes, some inorganic membranes offer extremely high H2/CO2 selectivity due to unique transport mechanisms [370], such as the proton-conductive Pd-based membranes and molecular  sieving  CMS  membranes.  Besides,  inorganic  membranes could work at higher temperature conditions and handle high pressures. However, inorganic membranes are currently too expensive for industrial  use  for  their  high  fabrication  cost  and  not  sufficient  long-term stability performances.
texts.text: Inorganic  membranes  such  as  metallic  membranes,  microporous ceramic membranes, carbon molecular sieving membranes, and dense ceramic  membranes  have  also  been  studied  for  H2/CO2 separation [369]. Compared to polymeric membranes, some inorganic membranes offer extremely high H2/CO2 selectivity due to unique transport mechanisms [370], such as the proton-conductive Pd-based membranes and molecular  sieving  CMS  membranes.  Besides,  inorganic  membranes could work at higher temperature conditions and handle high pressures. However, inorganic membranes are currently too expensive for industrial  use  for  their  high  fabrication  cost  and  not  sufficient  long-term stability performances.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pd-base membranes are one of the most intensively studied H2 separation membranes [371] as it only permeates H2, giving almost infinite H2/CO2 selectivity, which  could  offer an H2 purities of up  to 99 -99.995%,  a  purity  impossible  for  any  other  membranes  [372]. However, it is well-known that metallic H2  separation membranes are sensitive to poisoning by components such as CO and H2S; thus, a strict pre-treatment to remove these impurities is a must-have step to ensure long-term operation [373]. In recent years, three types of commercial Pd membranes have been applied in a 20 Nm 3 /h H2 production plant, and all  these  membranes  presented  good  stability  over  1300  h  [374].  In another  study,  Castro-Dominguez  et  al.  [375]  used  a  Pd  multitube membrane  module  for  coal-derived  syngas  separation.  The  module showed stable H2  permeance of 16.2 Nm 3  m 2  h 1 bar 1 throughout 840 h. The produced H2 purity was in the range of 98 -99.87% [375]. It is also reported that SINTEF and industrial partners aim to upscale a Pdbased membrane for purifying 25 -100 Nm 3 /h  of  H2  in  a  pilot  plant [376].

However, it is well-known that metallic H2  separation membranes are sensitive to poisoning by components such as CO and H2S; thus, a strict pre-treatment to remove these impurities is a must-have step to ensure long-term operation [373].
